content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA consisting of NNN and a target sum BBB.

Find the list of all unique combinations of the elements of AAA, such that the sum of the chosen elements equals BBB.

Note:


The same element may be chosen from the array AAA any number of times.
Two combinations are unique if the frequency of at least one of the chosen elements is different.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input:

The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and BBB - the size of the array and target sum, respectively.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers - the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output M+1M+1M+1 lines, where MMM is the number of unique combinations:



The first line contains a single integer MMM.
The next MMM lines contains a combination of space-separated elements of AAA which sums to BBB.

Note:



Elements in a combination (C1,C2,…,Ck)(C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k)(C1​,C2​,…,Ck​) must be printed in non-descending order, i.e., (C1≤C2≤…≤Ck)(C_1\le C_2\le \ldots\le C_k)(C1​≤C2​≤…≤Ck​).
The combinations must be printed in lexicographically increasing order.

We say that combination XXX is lexicographically smaller than YYY if either XXX is a prefix of YYY or there exists an index iii such that for all j<ij\lt ij<i, Xj=YjX_j = Y_jXj​=Yj​ and Xi<YiX_i\lt Y_iXi​<Yi​.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and B — the size of the array and the target sum. The second line contains N space‑separated integers representing the array A.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output M+1 lines where M is the number of unique combinations whose elements sum to B. The first line contains M. Each of the next M lines contains one combination: the chosen elements in non‑decreasing order separated by a single space. Combinations must be printed in lexicographically increasing order. If no combination exists, output a single line containing 0.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: only combination [2] sums to 2.\nTest case 2: combinations are [2,2,2,2], [2,3,3] and [3,5] which are printed in lexicographic order.\nTest case 3: only combination [2,2,3] sums to 7.
Array after removing duplicates is [2,3,5,7]. Combinations that sum to 10 are: [2,2,2,2,2], [2,2,3,3], [2,3,5] and [5,5]. They are already in lexicographic order.
No combination of the given numbers can produce sum 1, therefore M=0 and no further lines are printed.

solution.approach: For each test case we first sort the array and remove duplicate values because using the same value multiple times is allowed but duplicate values in the input would generate identical combinations. Then we run a depth‑first search (backtracking) that tries to build a combination in non‑decreasing order. The recursion keeps three parameters: the current index in the sorted unique array (to guarantee non‑decreasing order), the remaining sum needed, and the current partial combination. If the remaining sum becomes 0 we have found a valid combination and store it. If it becomes negative we backtrack. Because we always iterate from the current index forward, each combination is generated exactly once and the natural order of generation is lexicographic, so we can store them directly. After the search finishes we output the total count and the stored combinations.
solution.timeComplexity: The algorithm explores each valid combination once. In the worst case the number of recursive calls is O(K·L) where K is the number of combinations and L is the average length of a combination. With the given limits (N,B ≤ 20) this easily fits within time limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(L) for the recursion stack plus O(K·L) to store all combinations, where L is the maximum length of a combination (≤ B).
